natural_occurrence.description: A natural extract from the bark of the Quillaja saponaria tree containing saponins (soap-like compounds). Creates stable foam in beverages and acts as an emulsifier. Best known for creating the foam in root beer and cream soda.

manufacturing.description: The inner bark of Quillaja saponaria trees is harvested, dried, and extracted with hot water or dilute alcohol. The extract is filtered, concentrated, and purified to produce food-grade quillaia extract rich in saponins.

other_uses.cosmetics: Shampoos, foaming bath products, skin cleansers
other_uses.household: Natural soap and cleaning products, pet shampoos

eu.conditions: Authorized as foaming agent in beverages

usa.notes: Approved as foaming agent in beverages, max 200 ppm

canada.reference: Lists of Permitted Emulsifying, Gelling, Stabilizing or Thickening Agents
canada.notes: Approved as foaming agent, max 200 ppm in beverages

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: Not specified (use limited to 200 mg/kg in beverages)

regulatory_history.event: FDA affirms quillaia extract as GRAS for use in beverages
EFSA and JECFA re-evaluate quillaia extract and confirm safety at current use levels
Quillaia saponins investigated as COVID-19 vaccine adjuvant

equivalents.product: liters of root beer at max 200 ppm
equivalents.quantity: Daily intake well below levels of concern

adi_context.source_note: No ADI established - use levels are limited to 200 mg/kg in beverages. Typical exposure is low.

fun_facts: Makes root beer foam! That's its main claim to fame
Tree bark from Chile contains natural soap-like compounds (saponins)
Also used as a vaccine adjuvant to boost immune response
The name 'soapbark' comes from its traditional use as soap
Creates stable foam because saponins act like natural detergent
Also found in shampoos and natural cleaning products
Has been used in vaccine development, including COVID-19 vaccines
